source_paper_title: Improved Bounds for Single-Nomination Impartial Selection
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Cembrano_2025, title={Improved Bounds for Single-Nomination Impartial Selection}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0431}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0431}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Cembrano, Javier and Fischer, Felix and Klimm, Max}, year={2025}, month=jun }
context:
Page 14, Section 5 (end of the section introduction, immediately after stating that the upper bounds approach 3/4).

Like the bounds of Fischer and Klimm they approach \(3/4\) as \(n\to\infty\) and thus do not rule out the existence of an impartial mechanism that is close to \(3/4\)-optimal when \(n\) is large. Whether such a mechanism exists is an intriguing open question, the resolution of which is likely to require new insights.
title: Achieving three-quarters optimality for impartial selection in large single-nomination graphs
background_latex:
Let \(n\in\mathbb{N}\). Consider the single-nomination impartial selection setting represented by a directed graph \(G=(V,E)\) with \(V=\{1,\dots,n\}\), no self-loops, and outdegree exactly 1 for every vertex (each agent casts exactly one nomination): \(|\{u\in V:(v,u)\in E\}|=1\) for all \(v\in V\).

For a vertex \(v\in V\), its indegree is \(\delta^-(v,G)=|\{u\in V:(u,v)\in E\}|\). The maximum indegree in \(G\) is \(\Delta(G)=\max_{v\in V}\delta^-(v,G)\).

A (possibly randomized) selection mechanism is a map \(f\) that assigns to each such graph \(G\) a probability distribution \(f(G)\in[0,1]^n\) over vertices with \(\sum_{v\in V} f_v(G)=1\). The expected indegree of the selected vertex is
\[\mathbb{E}_{v\sim f(G)}[\delta^-(v,G)] = \sum_{v\in V} f_v(G)\,\delta^-(v,G).\]

The mechanism is called impartial if each vertex's selection probability does not depend on its own outgoing edge: for any two graphs \(G=(V,E)\) and \(G'=(V,E')\) on the same vertex set that differ only in \(v\)'s outgoing edge (formally, \(E\setminus(\{v\}\times V)=E'\setminus(\{v\}\times V)\)), one has \(f_v(G)=f_v(G')\).

For \(\alpha\le 1\), the mechanism \(f\) is \(\alpha\)-optimal on graphs with \(n\) vertices if for every graph \(G\) with \(\Delta(G)>0\),
\[\frac{\mathbb{E}_{v\sim f(G)}[\delta^-(v,G)]}{\Delta(G)}\ge \alpha.\]
Equivalently, the worst-case ratio \(\inf_{G:\Delta(G)>0} \mathbb{E}[\delta^-(v,G)]/\Delta(G)\) is at least \(\alpha\).

Known upper bounds in the paper imply that for each fixed \(n\ge 6\), any impartial \(\alpha\)-optimal mechanism on \(n\) vertices satisfies
\[\alpha \le \frac{3n^3-19n^2+30n-4}{4n(n-2)(n-4)},\]
which tends to \(3/4\) as \(n\to\infty\). The paper notes that these bounds do not exclude the possibility that the optimal achievable worst-case ratio approaches \(3/4\) as \(n\) grows.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a sequence of impartial selection mechanisms \(\{f^{(n)}\}_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\) for the single-nomination model (outdegree exactly 1) such that for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there is \(N\) with the property that for all \(n\ge N\) and all graphs \(G\) on \(n\) vertices with \(\Delta(G)>0\),
\[\frac{\mathbb{E}_{v\sim f^{(n)}(G)}[\delta^-(v,G)]}{\Delta(G)} \ge \frac{3}{4}-\varepsilon.\]
Equivalently, decide whether the supremum worst-case approximation ratio achievable by impartial mechanisms on \(n\)-vertex single-nomination graphs converges to \(3/4\) as \(n\to\infty\).

location_in_paper: Page 14, Section 5 (end of the section introduction, immediately after stating that the upper bounds approach 3/4).
open_problem_quote: Like the bounds of Fischer and Klimm they approach \(3/4\) as \(n\to\infty\) and thus do not rule out the existence of an impartial mechanism that is close to \(3/4\)-optimal when \(n\) is large. Whether such a mechanism exists is an intriguing open question, the resolution of which is likely to require new insights.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is existential/asymptotic over all impartial mechanisms, so a single finite counterexample instance cannot refute it outright; however, automated searches over small n could help falsify candidate mechanisms or suggest stronger upper-bound constructions.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate mechanisms can be represented algorithmically and empirically evaluated on generated graphs for small n, providing a fitness signal. But verifying asymptotic \(3/4-\varepsilon\) guarantees (or proving impossibility) requires analytical arguments beyond automatic evaluation.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
